primaryAngle: Edwardsville: from historic University District to new construction on the south and east sides

dominantEras: 1830s–1900s historic core (one of Illinois's oldest cities)
1990s–2020s subdivision growth on south and east sides
dominantStyles: Federal and Greek Revival on oldest blocks
Queen Anne and Victorian in the University District

neighborhoods.name: University District (St. Louis Street area)
neighborhoods.profile: Historic neighborhood adjacent to SIUE-area properties; significant 1880s–1930s housing stock. Many homes are owner-occupied; some are converted rental properties.

neighborhoods.name: Leclaire Historic District
neighborhoods.profile: Original company town of N.O. Nelson; nationally registered historic district with distinctive stone and frame homes.

neighborhoods.name: Glen Carbon corridor
neighborhoods.profile: Newer subdivision growth toward Glen Carbon, including HOA-governed master-planned communities.

neighborhoods.name: Eastern subdivisions
neighborhoods.profile: 1990s–2020s production housing on the city's eastern growth side; mostly asphalt-shingle inventory.

commonRoofingChallenges: Three-audience reality: historic homeowners, university-area landlords, new-construction owners — each with different priorities
Rental property roof scope tends toward 'function over premium' but landlord disclosure obligations still apply
Spring tornado and hail risk drives insurance-claim work
Historic district preservation considerations on Leclaire and University District properties
Glen Carbon and southeast growth corridor introduces HOA approval cycles for newer subdivisions
uniqueAngleElaboration: Edwardsville is one of Illinois's oldest cities — chartered in 1818 — and that history shows up in the housing stock around the University District and the nationally-registered Leclaire Historic District. But Edwardsville is also a fast-growing Metro East suburb, with significant subdivision build-out toward Glen Carbon and on the eastern side of town. That combination means roofing in Edwardsville serves three different homeowners: owners of historically significant properties who want material choices that respect the home's era, landlords with SIUE-area rental inventory where the priority is functional roofing on a tight budget, and new-construction or post-1990 subdivision owners working through HOA approvals. The contractor's job is to read which audience the homeowner falls into and tailor the conversation. Spring tornado and hail risk, common across the Metro East, drives a meaningful share of insurance-claim work in Edwardsville every year.
